does: This paper introduces TokenUnlearn, a token-level attribution framework for precise machine unlearning in large language models. Rather than applying uniform unlearning updates to entire sequences, the method identifies critical tokens encoding the knowledge to be forgotten using knowledge-aware signals via masking and entropy-aware signals to compute importance scores. The authors develop two complementary strategies: hard selection that applies unlearning only to high-importance tokens, and soft weighting that modulates gradient contributions proportionally to importance scores. Both strategies extend existing unlearning methods to operate at the token level. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that token-level selection improves the gradient signal-to-noise ratio compared to sequence-level approaches. Experiments on TOFU and WMDP benchmarks across three model architectures show consistent improvements in both forgetting effectiveness and utility preservation over sequence-level baselines.
connects: This work extends the emerging machine unlearning literature that addresses privacy and safety concerns in LLMs. Previous unlearning methods operated at the sequence level, applying gradient updates uniformly across entire sequences, similar to adversarial perturbation and knowledge distillation approaches. The paper builds on attribution and importance scoring techniques from interpretability research, which have identified how individual components contribute to model predictions. It also connects to recent work on fine-grained model editing and selective gradient-based optimization. The focus on balancing forgetting with utility preservation relates to prior work on catastrophic forgetting and continual learning. The use of masking-based signals draws from mechanistic interpretability research, while entropy-aware signals connect to information-theoretic approaches to understanding model behavior.
enables: TokenUnlearn points toward more precise control over what information models forget, enabling practical deployment of unlearning for regulatory compliance (GDPR, right-to-be-forgotten), removal of harmful training data, and fine-grained privacy protection. The token-level framework could inspire future work on causal tracing of knowledge in transformers and more surgical model editing techniques. This approach may enable selective unlearning of specific concepts or behaviors while preserving related capabilities, addressing a key challenge in safe model development. The theoretical insights about signal-to-noise ratios could inform optimization strategies for other selective model modification tasks. Furthermore, the framework could extend to multimodal models and support interpretability research by clarifying which tokens encode specific knowledge, potentially leading to better methods for auditing and controlling LLM behavior across safety-critical applications.
